questions.text: I believe that putting myself last is a natural expression of how I value others.
I tend to feel guilty when I spend a whole day on my own interests.
My emotional reactions are vivid enough to produce physical symptoms — headaches, stomach knots, trembling.
I find it important to stay aligned with the group, even at the cost of my personal preference.
I'm comfortable starting with incomplete information and figuring things out along the way.
I detect when a silence between people is comfortable versus strained.
I feel that my personal achievements are among the things I am most proud of.
I approach problems by first defining them clearly, then generating solutions systematically.
I find that I am more helpful to people in distress when I engage with their problem intellectually rather than emotionally.
I pick up on when a person glances at someone specific before responding to a question.
Small moments — a sunset, a kind word — can produce a surprisingly intense reaction in me.
I hold the view that giving genuine moral priority to suffering is the most consequential choice I make on a daily basis.
I often do not realize when a social norm has been violated until someone reacts.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the consideration that best reveals whether a person's ethical framework is serious or superficial.
I feel drawn to positions of formal authority.
I am quick to take ownership of a situation when things go sideways.
I believe that careful resource management today creates abundance tomorrow.
I notice that I can work through emotional challenges without losing productivity.
